name: MATHEMATICS
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2024

Code: 65/5/2 (set-2)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 38 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer book during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This Question paper contains 38 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Question paper is divided into FIVE Sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. In Section A - Question Number 1 to 18 are Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs) and Question Number 19 and 20 are Assertion-Reason based questions of 1 mark each.
  4. In Section B - Question Number 21 to 25 are Very Short Answer (VSA) type questions, carrying 2 marks each.
  5. In Section C - Question Number 26 to 31 are Short Answer (SA) type questions, carrying 3 marks each.
  6. In Section D - Question Number 32 to 35 are Long Answer (LA) type questions, carrying 5 marks each.
  7. In Section E - Question Number 36 to 38 are case study based questions, carrying 4 marks each.
  8. There is no overall choice. However, an internal choice has been provided in 2 questions in Section - B, 3 questions in Section - C, 2 questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{D}{/tex} and {tex}{2}{/tex} questions in Section - {tex}\boldsymbol{E}{/tex}.
  9. Use of calculators is NOT allowed.

SECTION-A

This section has {tex}{2 0}{/tex} multiple choice questions of {tex}{1}{/tex} mark each.
1.

Derivative of {tex}{e}^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex} with respect to {tex}\cos x{/tex} is :

a. {tex}\sin x {e}^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
b. {tex}\cos x {e}^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
c. {tex}-2 \cos x {e}^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
d. {tex}-2 \sin ^{2} x \cos x {e}^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}-2 \cos x {e}^{\sin ^{2} x}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}-2 \cos {e}^{\boldsymbol{s i n}^{2} \boldsymbol{x}}{/tex}

 1
2.

If A is a square matrix of order 2 and {tex}|{A}|=-2{/tex}, then value of {tex}\left|5 {~A}^{\prime}\right|{/tex} is :

a. -50
b. -10
c. 10
d. 50
Correct Answer: (a) -50
Explanation:

-50

 1
3.

The function {tex}{f}(x)=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{2}{x}{/tex} has a local minima at {tex}x{/tex} equal to :

a. 2
b. 1
c. 0
d. -2
Correct Answer: (a) 2
Explanation:

2

 1
4.

Given a curve {tex}{y}=7 x-x^{3}{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} increases at the rate of 2 units per second. The rate at which the slope of the curve is changing, when {tex}x=5{/tex} is :

a. -60 units/sec
b. 60 units/sec
c. -70 units/sec
d. -140 units/sec
Correct Answer: (a) -60 units/sec
Explanation:

-60 units/sec

 1
5.

The product of matrix P and Q is equal to a diagonal matrix. If the order of matrix Q is {tex}3 \times 2{/tex}, then order of matrix P is :

a. {tex}2 \times 2{/tex}
b. {tex}3 \times 3{/tex}
c. {tex}2 \times 3{/tex}
d. {tex}3 \times 2{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}2 \times 3{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}2 \times 3{/tex}

 1
6.

A function {tex}{f}: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}{/tex} defined as {tex}{f}(x)=x^{2}-4 x+5{/tex} is :

a. injective but not surjective.
b. surjective but not injective.
c. both injective and surjective.
d. neither injective nor surjective.
Correct Answer: (d) neither injective nor surjective.
Explanation:

neither injective nor surjective

7.

If {tex}\sin (x y)=1{/tex}, then {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex} is equal to :

a. {tex}\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
b. {tex}-\frac{x}{y}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{{y}}{x}{/tex}
d. {tex}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}-\frac{y}{x}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}-\frac{{y}}{{x}}{/tex}

8.

If inverse of matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex} is the matrix {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll}1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & \lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then value of {tex}\lambda{/tex} is :

a. {tex}-4{/tex}
b. 1
c. 3
d. 4
Correct Answer: (d) 4
Explanation:

4

9.

Find the matrix {tex}{A}^{2}{/tex}, where {tex}{A}=\left[{a}_{{ij}}\right]{/tex} is a {tex}2 \times 2{/tex} matrix whose elements are given by {tex}{a}_{{ij}}={/tex} maximum ( {tex}{i}, {j}{/tex} ) - minimum ( {tex}{i}, {j}{/tex} ) :

a. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
b. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}
c. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
d. {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]{/tex}

10.

If A is a square matrix of order 3 such that the value of {tex}|{adj} \cdot {A}|=8{/tex}, then the value of {tex}\left|{A}^{{T}}\right|{/tex} is :

a. {tex}\sqrt{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}-\sqrt{2}{/tex}
c. 8
d. {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}2 \sqrt{2}{/tex}

11.

The value of {tex}\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2} \cot \theta \operatorname{cosec}^{2} \theta {~d} \theta{/tex} is :

a. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
b. {tex}-\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
c. 0
d. {tex}-\frac{\pi}{8}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (a) {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

12.

The integral {tex}\int \frac{{d} x}{\sqrt{9-4 x^{2}}}{/tex} is equal to :

a. {tex}\frac{1}{6} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{3}\right)+{c}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{3}\right)+{c}{/tex}
c. {tex}\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{3}\right)+{c}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{3}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{3}\right)+{c}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{3}\right)+{c}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{3}\right)+c{/tex}

13.

The area of the region bounded by the curve {tex}y^{2}=4 x{/tex} and {tex}x=1{/tex} is :

a. {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{8}{3}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{64}{3}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{32}{3}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) {tex}\frac{8}{3}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{8}{3}{/tex}

14.

The general solution of the differential equation
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}={e}^{x+{y}} \text { is }:{/tex}

a. {tex}{e}^{x}+{e}^{-{y}}={c}{/tex}
b. {tex}{e}^{-x}+{e}^{-{y}}={c}{/tex}
c. {tex}{e}^{x+{y}}={c}{/tex}
d. {tex}2 {e}^{x+{y}}={c}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (a) {tex}{e}^{x}+{e}^{-{y}}={c}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}{e}^{{x}}+{e}^{-{y}}={c}{/tex}

15.

The angle which the line {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{y}{-1}=\frac{z}{0}{/tex} makes with the positive direction of Y-axis is :

a. {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{6}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{5 \pi}{4}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{7 \pi}{4}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (b) {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}

16.

The Cartesian equation of the line passing through the point {tex}(1,-3,2){/tex} and parallel to the line :
{tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}=(2+\lambda) \hat{{i}}+\lambda \hat{{j}}+(2 \lambda-1) \hat{{k}}{/tex} is

a. {tex}\frac{x-1}{2}=\frac{y+3}{0}=\frac{z-2}{-1}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{x+1}{1}=\frac{y-3}{1}=\frac{z+2}{2}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{x+1}{2}=\frac{y-3}{0}=\frac{z+2}{-1}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+3}{1}=\frac{z-2}{2}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+3}{1}=\frac{z-2}{2}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\frac{x-1}{1}=\frac{y+3}{1}=\frac{z-2}{2}{/tex}

17.

If A and B are events such that {tex}{P}({A} / {B})={P}({B} / {A}) \neq 0{/tex}, then :

a. {tex}{A} \subset {B}{/tex}, but {tex}{A} \neq {B}{/tex}
b. {tex}{A}={B}{/tex}
c. {tex}{A} \cap {B}=\phi{/tex}
d. {tex}{P}({A})={P}({B}){/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}{P}({A})={P}({B}){/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}{P}({A})={P}({B}){/tex}

18.

The position vectors of points {tex}P{/tex} and {tex}Q{/tex} are {tex}\vec{p}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{q}{/tex} respectively. The point R divides line segment PQ in the ratio {tex}3: 1{/tex} and S is the mid-point of line segment PR. The position vector of {tex}S{/tex} is :

a. {tex}\frac{\vec{p}+3 \vec{q}}{4}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{\vec{p}+3 \vec{q}}{8}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{5 \vec{p}+3 \vec{q}}{4}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{5 \vec{p}+3 \vec{q}}{8}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (d) {tex}\frac{5 \vec{p}+3 \vec{q}}{8}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex}\frac{5 \vec{p}+3 \vec{q}}{8}{/tex}

 1

Assertion - Reason Based Questions

Direction : In questions numbers 19 and 20, two statements are given one labelled Assertion (A) and the other labelled Reason (R). Select the correct answer from the following options :

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and the Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
19.

Assertion (A): The vectors
{tex}\vec{a}=6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-8 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{b}=10 \hat{i}-2 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{c}=4 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
represent the sides of a right angled triangle.
Reason (R): Three non-zero vectors of which none of two are collinear forms a triangle if their resultant is zero vector or sum of any two vectors is equal to the third.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
Correct Answer: (b) Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Explanation:

Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

 1
20.

Assertion (A): Domain of {tex}{y}=\cos ^{-1}(x){/tex} is {tex}[-1,1]{/tex}.
Reason (R): The range of the principal value branch of {tex}{y}=\cos ^{-1}(x){/tex} is {tex}[0, \pi]-\left\{\frac{\pi}{2}\right\}{/tex}.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
Correct Answer: (c) A is true but R is false.
Explanation:

A is true but R is false.

 1

SECTION-B

This section has 5 Very Short Answer questions of 2 marks each.
21.

If {tex}{a}=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)+\cos ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} and
{tex}{b}=\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{3})-\cot ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right){/tex}
then find the value of {tex}{a}+{b}{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}a=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{2 \pi}{3}, b=\frac{\pi}{3}-\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore a+b=\frac{7 \pi}{12}{/tex}

 2
22.(i)

Find : {tex}\int {{{\cos }^3}x.{e^{\log \sin x}}dx} {/tex}

Solution:

{tex}\int {{{\cos }^3}x.{e^{\log \sin x}}dx} {/tex}

{tex}\because {e^{\log \theta }} = \theta {/tex}

{tex}\therefore {e^{\log \sin x}} = \sin x{/tex}

{tex}= \int {{{\cos }^3}x.\sin x}{/tex}

put {tex}\cos x = t{/tex}

{tex}- \sin xdx = dt{/tex}

{tex}\sin xdx = - dt{/tex}

{tex} = \int { - {t^3}dt} {/tex}

{tex} = - \frac{{{t^4}}}{4} + c = - \frac{{{{\cos }^4}x}}{4} + c{/tex}

 2

OR
(ii)

Find : {tex}\int \frac{1}{5+4 x-x^{2}} {~d} x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}\int \frac{1}{5+4 x-x^{2}} d x=\int \frac{1}{3^{2}-(x-2)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{6} \log \left\|\frac{1+x}{5-x}\right\|+C{/tex}

 2
23.

Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of {tex}15 {~cm}^{3} /{/tex} minute. The falling sand forms a cone on the ground such that the height of the cone is always onethird of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing at the instant when the height is 4 cm?

Solution:

Let ' {tex}V{/tex} ' be the volume, '{tex}h{/tex}' and '{tex}r{/tex}' be the height and radius of the cone,
{tex}\therefore h=\frac{r}{3}, \frac{d V}{d t}=15 {~cm}^{3} / {min}{/tex}
{tex}V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^{2} h=3 \pi h^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d}{d t}\left(\frac{\pi}{3} r^2 h\right)=15 \Rightarrow 3 \pi \frac{d\left(h^3\right)}{d t}=15 \Rightarrow \frac{d h}{d t}\right]_{h=4}{/tex}{tex}\left.=\frac{5}{3 \pi {~h}^2}\right]_{{hm}=4}=\frac{5}{48 \pi} {~cm} / {min}{/tex}

 2
24.

Find the vector equation of the line passing through the point {tex}(2,3,-5){/tex} and making equal angles with the co-ordinate axes.

Solution:

{tex}\cos \alpha=\cos \beta=\cos \gamma=l \Rightarrow l^{2}+l^{2}+l^{2}=1 {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3 l^{2}=1, \therefore l=1 / \sqrt{3}{/tex}
Direction cosines of the line are {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow{/tex} the direction ratios are 1,1,1
∴ Vector equation of the line is: {tex}\overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}+3 \hat{{j}}-5 \hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}){/tex}

 2
25.(i)

Verify whether the function f defined by
{tex}f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} x \sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right), & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0 \end{array}\right.{/tex}
is continuous at {tex}x=0{/tex} or not.

Solution:

{tex}\lim _{x \rightarrow 0}(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x . \sin \frac{1}{x}=0 \times{/tex} Finite value in {tex}[-1,1]=0=f(0){/tex}
{tex}\therefore {f}{/tex} is a continuous function.

 2

OR
(ii)

Check for differentiability of the function f defined by {tex}{f}(x)=|x-5|{/tex}, at the point {tex}x=5{/tex}.

Solution:

LHD {tex}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{-}} \frac{-(x-5)}{x-5}=-1{/tex}
{tex}R H D=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{\|x-5\|-0}{x-5}=\lim \limits_{x \rightarrow 5^{+}} \frac{(x-5)}{x-5}=1{/tex}
LHD {tex}\neq{/tex} {tex}\boldsymbol{} {R H D ,} \therefore {f}{/tex} is not differentiable at {tex}{x}={5}{/tex}

 2

SECTION-C

There are {tex}{6}{/tex} short answer questions in this section. Each is of {tex}{3}{/tex} marks.
26.(i)

Find the particular solution of the differential equation
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}-2 x {y}=3 x^{2} {e}^{x^{2}} ; {y}(0)=5{/tex}

Solution:

Given differential equation is a linear order differential equation with:
{tex}P=-2 x, Q=3 x^2 e^{x^2}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Integrating Factor }=e^{\int-2 x d x}=e^{-x^2}{/tex}
The general solution is: {tex}y \cdot e^{-x^2}=\int e^{-x^2} \cdot 3 x^2 e^{x^2} d x+C \Rightarrow y \cdot e^{-x^2}=x^3+C{/tex}
Putting {tex}x=0, y=5{/tex}, we get, {tex}C=5{/tex}
∴ The Particular solution is: {tex}{y} \cdot {e}^{-{x}^{{2}}}={x}^{{3}}+5{/tex} or {tex}{y}=\left({x}^{{3}}+5\right) {e}^{{x}^{{2}}}{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

Solve the following differential equation :
{tex}x^{2} d y+y(x+y) d x=0{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}x^2 d y+y(x+y) d x=0 \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{y}{x}-\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)^2{/tex}
Putting {tex}\frac{y}{x}=v \Rightarrow y=v x, \frac{d y}{d x}=v+x \frac{d v}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}v+x \frac{d v}{d x}=-v-v^2,{/tex}
separating the variable and integrating
{tex}\int \frac{1}{v^2+2 v} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(v+1)^2-1} d v=-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \log \left|\frac{v}{v+2}\right|=\log \left|\frac{C}{x}\right|{/tex}
The solution of the differential equation is, {tex}\left\|\frac{y}{y+2 x}\right\|=\frac{C^{2}}{x^{2}}{/tex} or {tex}x^{2} y=k(y+2 x){/tex} 

 3
27.

Find the values of a and b so that the following function is differentiable for all values of {tex}x{/tex} :
{tex}{f}(x)= \begin{cases}{a} x+{b}, & x \gt -1 \\ {~b} x^{2}-3, & x \leq-1\end{cases}{/tex}

Solution:

Since Differentiability ⇒ Continuity
{tex}f(x){/tex} is continuous at {tex}x=-1 \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-^{-}}\left(b x^{2}-3\right)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}}(a x+b){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow b-3=-a+b, \therefore a=3{/tex}
{tex}f(x){/tex} is differentiable at {tex}x=-1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} \frac{\left(b x^{2}-3\right)-(b-3)}{x+1}=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{(a x+b)-(b-3)}{x+1}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{-}} b(x-1)=\lim _{x \rightarrow-1^{+}} \frac{3(x+1)}{x+1}, \therefore b=-\frac{3}{2}{/tex}
Alternately,
{tex}L H D=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(b-3)-\left(b(-1-h)^{2}-3\right)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(-b h-2 b)=-2 b{/tex}
{tex}{R H D}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(-1)-f(-1-h)}{h}={/tex} {tex}\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{[a(-1+h)+b]-(b-3)}{h}=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{(-a+3)+a h}{h}=a{/tex}
{tex}\text { and }-a+3=0 \Rightarrow a=3,-2 b=a \Rightarrow b=-3 / 2{/tex}

 3
28.(i)

Find {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}{/tex}, if {tex}(\cos x)^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{x}{/tex}.

Solution:

Taking 'log' on both sides of {tex}(\cos {x})^{{y}}=(\cos {y})^{\boldsymbol{x}}{/tex}, we get
{tex}{y} \log \cos {x}={x} \log \cos {y}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} \log \cos x+y(-\tan x){/tex} {tex}=\log \cos y+x(-\tan y) \frac{d y}{d x}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}}=\frac{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {y}+{y} \tan {x}}{\boldsymbol{\operatorname { l o g }} \cos {x}+{x} \tan {y}}{/tex}

 3

OR
(ii)

If {tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}{/tex} = a(x - y), then prove that {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex}.

Solution:

{tex}\sqrt{1-x^2}+\sqrt{1-y^2}=a(x-y){/tex}
Let x = sin A, y = sin B
{tex}\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 A}+\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 B}{/tex} = a(sin A - sin B)
cos A + cos B = a(sin A - sin B)
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}=2 a \cos \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right) \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cos \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a \sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \cot \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)=a{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{A-B}{2}=\cot ^{-1} a{/tex}
A - B = 2 cot-1 a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} sin-1 x - sin-1y = 2 cot-1 a
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Differentiating w.r.t. x
{tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-y^2}} \frac{d y}{d x}=0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{\sqrt{1-y^2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{/tex}

 3
29.(i)

Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex}

Solution:

According to the question , {tex}I=\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex} ........(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } } { e ^ { \cos ( \pi - x ) } + e ^ { - \cos ( \pi - x ) } } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int_0^af(x) = \int _0^a f(a-x)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { - \cos x } + e ^ { \cos x } } d x{/tex}.....(ii)
On adding Equation (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } { e ^ { \cos x } + e ^ { - \cos x } } d x{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } 1 d x{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow 2 I = [ x ] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi }{/tex}
{tex}=\pi - 0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 2I=\pi {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \pi / 2{/tex}

{tex}\therefore I = \pi / 2{/tex} sq units.

 3

OR
(ii)

Find : {tex}\int \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^{2}(x-1)} {d} x{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}\int \frac{2 x+1}{(x+1)^2(x-1)} d x =-\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{1}{x+1} d x{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{1}{(x+1)^2} d x+\frac{3}{4} \int \frac{1}{x-1} d x{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{3}{4} \log |x+1|-\frac{1}{2(x+1)}+\frac{3}{4} \log |x-1|+C{/tex}
or, {tex}=\frac{3}{4} \log \left|\frac{x-1}{x+1}\right|-\frac{1}{2(x+1)}+C{/tex}

 3
30.

Given {tex}\vec{a}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}=3 \hat{i}-\hat{k}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c}=2 \hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}{/tex}. Find a vector {tex}\vec{d}{/tex} which is perpendicular to both {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{c} \cdot \vec{d}=3{/tex}.

Solution:

Since {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{a}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{{d}} \perp \overrightarrow{{b}} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\overrightarrow{{a}} \times \overrightarrow{{b}}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{d}}=\lambda(\hat{{i}}+5 \hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
{tex}\overrightarrow{{c}} \cdot \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \Rightarrow 2 \lambda+5 \lambda-6 \lambda=3 \Rightarrow \lambda=3{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{{d}}=3 \hat{{i}}+{1 5} \hat{{j}}+{9} \hat{{k}}{/tex}

 3
31.

Bag I contains 3 red and 4 black balls, Bag II contains 5 red and 2 black balls. Two balls are transferred at random from Bag I to Bag II and then a ball is drawn at random from Bag II. Find the probability that the drawn ball is red in colour.

Solution:

E1: Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red.
E2: Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Black.
E3: Two Balls transferred from Bag I are Red and Black.
A: Ball drawn from Bag II is Red
{tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)={/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{2}{7}, {P}\left({E}_{3}\right)=\frac{4}{7}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} {tex}=\frac{7}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=\frac{5}{9}, {P}\left({~A} \mid {E}_{3}\right)=\frac{6}{9}{/tex}
{tex}{P}({~A})=\frac{1}{7} \cdot \frac{7}{9}+\frac{2}{7} \cdot \frac{5}{9}+\frac{4}{7} \cdot \frac{6}{9}=\frac{41}{63}{/tex}

 3

SECTION-D

There are 4 long answer questions in this section. Each question is of 5 marks.
32.(i)

Find the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point {tex}(2,3,-8){/tex} to the line {tex}\frac{4-x}{2}=\frac{y}{6}=\frac{1-z}{3}{/tex}.
Also, find the perpendicular distance of the given point from the line.

Solution:

We have equation of line is {tex}\frac{{4 - x}}{2} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{1 - z}}{3}{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 4}}{{ - 2}} = \frac{y}{6} = \frac{{z - 1}}{{ - 3}} = \lambda {/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow x = - 2\lambda + 4,y = 6\lambda {/tex} and {tex}z = - 3\lambda + 1{/tex}

Let the coordinates of L be {tex}(4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda ){/tex}, then, direction ratios of PL are proportional to {tex}(4 - 2\lambda - 2,6\lambda - 3,1 - 3\lambda + 8){/tex} i.e., {tex}(2 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda - 3,9 - 3\lambda ){/tex}.
Also, direction ratios are proportional to -2, 6, -3. Since, PL is perpendicular to give line.
{tex}\therefore - 2(2 - 2\lambda ) + 6(6\lambda - 3) - 3(9 - 3\lambda ) = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow - 4 + 4\lambda + 36\lambda - 18 - 27 + 9\lambda = 0{/tex}
{tex} \Rightarrow 49\lambda = 49 \Rightarrow \lambda = 1{/tex}
So, the coordinates of L are {tex}\left( {4 - 2\lambda ,6\lambda ,1 - 3\lambda } \right){/tex} i.e., (2, 6, -2).

Also, length of PL {tex} = \sqrt {{{(2 - 2)}^2} + {{(6 - 3)}^2} + {{( - 2 + 8)}^2}} {/tex}
{tex} = \sqrt {0 + 9 + 36} = 3\sqrt 5 units{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

Find the shortest distance between the lines {tex}{L}_{1} \& {~L}_{2}{/tex} given below :
{tex}{L}_{1}{/tex} : The line passing through {tex}(2,-1,1){/tex} and parallel to {tex}\frac{x}{1}=\frac{{y}}{1}=\frac{{z}}{3}{/tex}
{tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=\hat{{i}}+(2 \mu+1) \hat{{j}}-(\mu+2) \hat{{k}}{/tex}

Solution:

Equation of {tex}{L}_{1}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=2 \hat{{i}}-\hat{{j}}+\hat{{k}}+\lambda(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}+3 \hat{{k}}){/tex}
Equation of {tex}{L}_{2}: \overrightarrow{{r}}=(\hat{{i}}+\hat{{j}}-2 \hat{{k}})+\mu(2 \hat{{j}}-\hat{{k}}){/tex}
Taking {tex}\vec{a}_{1}=2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+\hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}=\hat{i}+\hat{j}-2 \hat{k}_{,} \vec{b}_{2}=2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}=-\hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}, \vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}=-7 \hat{i}+\hat{j}+2 \hat{k}{/tex}
{tex}\text { Shortest Distance }=\frac{\left\|\left(\vec{a}_{2}-\vec{a}_{1}\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right)\right\|}{\left\|\vec{b}_{1} \times \vec{b}_{2}\right\|}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}{/tex}

 5
(i)

If {tex}{A}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 0 & -3 \\ 1 & 2 & 0\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then find {tex}{A}^{-1}{/tex} and hence solve the following system of equations :
{tex}x+2 y-3 z=1{/tex}
{tex}2 x-3 z=2{/tex}
{tex}x+2 y=3{/tex}

Solution:

{tex}\|A\|=1(6)-2(3)-3(4)=-12 \neq 0, \therefore A^{-1}{/tex} exist
{tex} \operatorname{adj} A =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex} \therefore A^{-1} =-\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right] {/tex}
The given system of equations can be written as {tex}{A X}={B}, {X}=\left[\begin{array}{l}{x} \\ {y} \\ {z}\end{array}\right], {B}=\left[\begin{array}{l}1 \\ 2 \\ 3\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}X=A^{-1} B \Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{12}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & -6 & -6 \\ -3 & 3 & -3 \\ 4 & 0 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 1 / 2 \\ 2 / 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}The solution of the given system of equations is: {tex}x=2, y=\frac{1}{2}, z=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

 5

OR
(ii)

Find the product of the matrices {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc}-6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1\end{array}\right]{/tex} and hence solve the system of linear equations:
{tex}x+2 y-3 z=-4{/tex}
{tex}2 x+3 y+2 z=2{/tex}
{tex}3 x-3 y-4 z=11{/tex}

Solution:

{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right] } {/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 67 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 67 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 67 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
 {tex}\Rightarrow {\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]^{-1}=\frac{1}{67}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right] } {/tex} 
Solution of the system of equations is given by:
{tex} {\left[\begin{array}{l} x \\ y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 2 & -3 \\ 2 & 3 & 2 \\ 3 & -3 & -4 \end{array}\right]^{-1}\left[\begin{array}{c} -4 \\ 2 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]=} {/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{67}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 17 & 13 \\ 14 & 5 & -8 \\ -15 & 9 & -1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} -4 \\ 2 \\ 11 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} 3 \\ -2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right], {/tex}
{tex} \therefore x=3, y=-2, z=1 {/tex}

 5
34.

Find the area of the region bounded by the curve {tex}4 x^{2}+y^{2}=36{/tex} using integration.

Solution:

The given equation can be written as: {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{9}+\frac{y^{2}}{36}=1{/tex}, which is an ellipse.

Area of the region bounded
{tex}=4 \times \frac{6}{3} \int_{0}^{3} \sqrt{9-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=8\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{9-x^{2}}+\frac{9}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{3}\right]_{0}^{3}{/tex}
{tex}=18 \pi{/tex}

 5
35.

Solve the following Linear Programming problem graphically :
Maximise {tex}{Z}=300 {x}+600 {y}{/tex}
Subject to {tex}x+2 {y} \leq 12{/tex}
{tex}2 x+y \leq 12{/tex}
{tex}x+\frac{5}{4} y \geq 5{/tex}
{tex}x \geq 0, y \geq 0{/tex}

Solution:

Corner Points Value of {tex}Z=300 x+600 y{/tex}
{tex}A(0,4){/tex} {tex}Z=2400{/tex}
{tex}B(0,6){/tex} {tex}Z=3600{/tex}
{tex}C(4,4){/tex} {tex}Z=3600{/tex}
{tex}D(6,0){/tex} {tex}Z=1800{/tex}
{tex}E(5,0){/tex} {tex}Z=1500{/tex}

{tex}\operatorname{Max}(Z)=3600{/tex}, at all points on the line segment {tex}B C{/tex}

SECTION-E

In this section there are {tex}{3}{/tex} case study questions of {tex}{4}{/tex} marks each.
36.

A departmental store sends bills to charge its customers once a month. Past experience shows that {tex}70 \%{/tex} of its customers pay their first month bill in time. The store also found that the customer who pays the bill in time has the probability of 0.8 of paying in time next month and the customer who doesn't pay in time has the probability of 0.4 of paying in time the next month.
Based on the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Let {tex}{E}_{1}{/tex} and {tex}{E}_{2}{/tex} respectively denote the event of customer paying or not paying the first month bill in time.
    Find {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right), {P}\left({E}_{2}\right){/tex}. (1)
  2. Let A denotes the event of customer paying second month's bill in time, then find {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right){/tex} and {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the probability of customer paying second month's bill in time. (2)

      OR

    2. Find the probability of customer paying first month's bill in time if it is found that customer has paid the second month's bill in time. (2)
Solution:
  1. {tex}{P}\left({E}_{1}\right)=\frac{7}{10}=0.7, {P}\left({E}_{2}\right)=\frac{3}{10}=0.3{/tex}
  2. {tex}{P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)=0.8, {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)=0.4{/tex}
    1. {tex}{P}({A})={P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right){/tex} = 0.7 {tex}\times 0.8+0.3 \times 0.4=0.68{/tex} or {tex}\frac{17}{25}{/tex}
      OR
    2. {tex}{P}({A})=\frac{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)}{{P}\left({E}_{1}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{1}\right)+{P}\left({E}_{2}\right) \cdot {P}\left({A} \mid {E}_{2}\right)}=\frac{14}{17}{/tex}
 4
37.

Students of a school are taken to a railway museum to learn about railways heritage and its history.

An exhibit in the museum depicted many rail lines on the track near the railway station. Let L be the set of all rail lines on the railway track and R be the relation on L defined by
{tex}{R}=\left\{\left(l_{1}, l_{2}\right): l_{1}\right.{/tex} is parallel to {tex}\left.l_{2}\right\}{/tex}
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. Find whether the relation R is symmetric or not. (1)
  2. Find whether the relation R is transitive or not. (1)
    1. If one of the rail lines on the railway track is represented by the equation {tex}{y}=3 x+2{/tex}, then find the set of rail lines in R related to it. (2)

      Or

    2. Let S be the relation defined by {tex}{S}=\left\{\left(l_{1}, l_{2}\right): l_{1}\right.{/tex} is perpendicular to {tex}\left.l_{2}\right\}{/tex} check whether the relation S is symmetric and transitive. (2)
Solution:
  1. Let {tex}\left(l_{1}, l_{2}\right) \in R \Rightarrow l_{1}\left\\|l_{2} \Rightarrow l_{2}\right\\| l_{1} \Rightarrow\left(l_{2}, l_{1}\right) \in R, \therefore R{/tex} is a symmetric relation
  2. Let {tex}\left(l_1, l_2\right),\left(l_2, l_3\right) \in R \Rightarrow l_1\left\|l_2, l_2\right\| l_3 \Rightarrow l_1 \| l_3 \Rightarrow\left(l_1, l_3\right) \in R, \therefore R{/tex} is a transitive relation
    1. The set is {tex}\{1: 1{/tex} is a line of type {tex}y=3 x+c, c \in R\}{/tex}

      Or

    2. Let {tex}\left(l_{1}, l_{2}\right) \in R \Rightarrow l_{1} \perp l_{2} \Rightarrow l_{2} \perp l_{1} \Rightarrow\left(l_{2}, l_{1}\right) \in R, \therefore R{/tex} is a symmetric relationLet {tex}\left(l_1, l_2\right),\left(l_2, l_3\right) \in R \Rightarrow l_1 \perp l_2, l_2 \perp l_3{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow l_1 \| l_3 \Rightarrow\left(l_1, l_3\right) \notin R, \therefore R{/tex} is not a transitive relation
 4
38.

A rectangular visiting card is to contain 24 sq.cm. of printed matter. The margins at the top and bottom of the card are to be 1 cm and the margins on the left and right are to be {tex}1 \frac{1}{2} {~cm}{/tex} as shown below :


On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. Write the expression for the area of the visiting card in terms of {tex}x{/tex}. (2)
  2. Obtain the dimensions of the card of minimum area. (2)
Solution:
  1. Let {tex}{A}({x}){/tex} be the area of the visiting card then,
    {tex}\text { As } x y=24, A(x)=(x+3)(y+2)=2 x+3 y+x y+6=2 x+\frac{72}{x}+30{/tex}
  2. {tex}{A}^{\prime}({x})=2-\frac{72}{{x}^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}({x})=\frac{144}{{x}^{3}}{/tex},
    solving {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=6{/tex} is the critical point. {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(6)=\frac{144}{6} \gt 0,{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the card is minimum at } x=6, y=4{/tex}
    The dimension of the card with minimum area is Length {tex}=9 {~cm}{/tex}, Breadth {tex}=6 {~cm}{/tex}
 4
